ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general shrimp food product class; no specific grilled shrimp skewer term exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CJW covers shrimps and prawns as a category; no specific grilled skewer preparation code exists. closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 227144002 is the closest available substance code for shrimp; no grilled skewer preparation concept exists.

main_ingredients: marinade (garlic, soy sauce, sesame oil, spices)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of grilled shrimp skewers consists of 5 skewers, each holding 2–3 medium shrimp, totalling approximately 150 g of cooked shrimp flesh.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, moist heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15152)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp values used as the primary nutrient base. Sodium adjusted upward by approximately 15% to account for typical soy-sauce-based marinade used in Chinese-style grilled shrimp skewers. All other macros and micronutrients retained from USDA cooked shrimp data. This is the closest defensible approved-source match; no Singapore HPB Foodo entry for grilled shrimp skewers was identified.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for grilled shrimp skewers. Shrimp and other lean seafood are very low in digestible carbohydrate (approximately 1 g per 100 g cooked). GI is estimated at 5 based on analogous low-carbohydrate protein foods (cooked fish and shellfish) referenced in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (5 × 1.5 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.075, rounded to 0.1. gi_category is low.

hypertension.guidance: Each 150 g serving provides approximately 570 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium marinade to stay within the 2,000 mg daily sodium target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only approximately 1.5 g of carbohydrate per 150 g serving and a GI of approximately 5, grilled shrimp skewers have a negligible glycemic impact and are a suitable high-protein, low-GI food choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol content and sodium from marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Shrimp is relatively high in dietary cholesterol (approximately 284 mg per 150 g serving) but low in saturated fat; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion frequency and choose low-sodium marinade preparations to reduce overall cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Grilled shrimp skewers (5 pieces) are a popular Chinese-style street food and restaurant dish consisting of medium to large shrimp threaded onto bamboo or metal skewers, marinated in a mixture of garlic, soy sauce, sesame oil, and spices, then grilled over charcoal or a gas flame until lightly charred and cooked through. Each serving of five skewers weighs approximately 150 g. The texture is firm and slightly springy, with a smoky, savoury, mildly sweet flavour from the marinade caramelising during grilling. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found at Chinese barbecue stalls, hawker centres, and casual dining restaurants such as Lan Ting Xu. Nutritionally, grilled shrimp skewers are an excellent source of lean protein (approximately 34 g per serving), low in fat and carbohydrate, and provide meaningful amounts of selenium, phosphorus, vitamin B12, and cholesterol. The glycemic index is very low (approximately 5), making this dish suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate due to the soy-sauce marinade, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is naturally gluten-free if tamari is substituted for soy sauce, and is suitable for halal diets when prepared with compliant ingredients.
common_user_queries: How many calories in shrimp skewers?
How much protein in prawn skewers?
Are shrimp skewers high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry for grilled shrimp skewers was identified. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp (NDB 15152) with a minor sodium adjustment for marinade. GI is estimated by analogy from approved University of Sydney GI Database reference data for low-carbohydrate seafood. Confidence is medium rather than high because the source match is adapted rather than exact.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are based on USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp with sodium adjusted upward by approximately 15% to account for soy-sauce marinade typical of Chinese-style grilled shrimp skewers sold in Singapore. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked shrimp; shrimp contains no industrially produced trans fats. 3. sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for plain cooked shrimp; trace sugars from marinade are below detection threshold. 4. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; shrimp contains no dietary fibre. 5. GI is estimated at 5 based on analogous low-carbohydrate protein foods from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for grilled shrimp skewers was found in approved sources. 6. The input food_name 'render shrimp skewers (5pcs)' appears to be a transliteration artefact from a Chinese menu listing; the canonical English name has been normalised to 'Grilled Shrimp Skewers (5 pieces)'. 7. Serving size of 150 g is estimated based on 5 medium shrimp skewers at approximately 30 g per skewer. 8. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as the dish is served in Singapore. 9. All other nutrient values (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g) are reported from USDA SR Legacy cooked shrimp data.
